skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Establishes a disciplined workflow for discovering and invoking relevant skills before any AI response or action, ensuring consistent use of marketplace skills
skill.summary: A meta-skill that enforces skill discovery and invocation before any task execution, preventing undisciplined shortcuts
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All five static analysis findings are false positives. The pattern scanner incorrectly flagged Markdown formatting (backticks for inline code at line 18, code blocks containing DOT graph syntax at line 28) as shell commands. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections at lines 3, 74, and 92 matched text substrings in YAML frontmatter and section headers, not actual cryptographic code. This skill is purely instructional documentation with no executable code, external commands, or security-sensitive operations.

content.user_title: Enforce skill discovery before every task
content.value_statement: AI agents often skip helpful skills by rationalizing that a task is too simple or urgent. This skill establishes a mandatory checkpoint to discover and invoke relevant skills before any action, preventing wasted effort from undisciplined shortcuts.
actual_capabilities: Provides a decision flowchart for when to check for relevant skills
Defines priority ordering when multiple skills could apply
Lists common rationalizations that lead to skipping useful skills
Distinguishes between rigid skills (follow exactly) and flexible skills (adapt principles)
Establishes the Skill tool as the primary mechanism for skill discovery in Claude Code
limitations: Requires AI agent to have access to a Skill tool or equivalent skill discovery mechanism
Designed primarily for Claude Code and similar AI coding environments
Does not provide automatic skill detection - relies on agent discipline to check
May introduce latency for simple tasks where skill lookup seems unnecessary

use_cases.title: New project kickoff
use_cases.description: When starting work on an unfamiliar codebase or task type, this skill ensures you discover relevant workflow skills before diving in.
use_cases.target_user: Developers working with AI coding assistants on new projects

use_cases.title: Team skill standardization
use_cases.description: Teams can adopt this as a baseline discipline to ensure consistent use of organizational skills across all AI-assisted work.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering managers establishing AI workflow standards

use_cases.title: Skill marketplace integration
use_cases.description: Users exploring the AI skill marketplace can use this as their first skill to discover other relevant skills for their workflow.
use_cases.target_user: AI power users building personalized skill collections

prompt_templates.title: Basic skill check
prompt_templates.prompt: Before responding, check if any available skills apply to this request. If a skill might be relevant (even 1% chance), invoke it using the Skill tool and announce which skill you are using and why.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-skill workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to build a new feature. Following the using-superpowers skill: first check for process skills (like brainstorming or planning), then check for implementation skills. Invoke each relevant skill in order and follow their guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Bug fixing discipline
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me debug this issue. Before suggesting any fixes, invoke any debugging or troubleshooting skills that apply. Follow the skill's checklist if one is provided.

prompt_templates.title: Skill-aware clarification
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a question about my project structure. Before asking me clarifying questions, check if any skills define how to explore or understand codebases. Invoke relevant skills first, then follow their guidance on what information to gather.

output_examples.input: User asks: Can you help me add a login form to my React app?
output: I'll help you add a login form. Following the using-superpowers skill, let me first check for relevant skills.
Using frontend-design skill to guide the UI implementation approach.
Using react-patterns skill to ensure proper component structure.
Now that I understand the recommended approaches, let me ask: Do you have existing authentication infrastructure, or should we design a complete solution?

output_examples.input: User asks: Why is my build failing?
output: I'll help debug your build failure. Following the using-superpowers skill, I'm invoking the debugging skill first to ensure a systematic approach.
The debugging skill requires me to: (1) reproduce the error, (2) gather the full error output, (3) check recent changes.
Can you share the complete build error message and any recent changes to your build configuration?

best_practices: Check for skills immediately upon receiving any user message - before forming a response, before asking clarifying questions, before taking any action
When multiple skills apply, invoke process skills (brainstorming, debugging, planning) before implementation skills (specific frameworks or tools)
Announce which skill you are invoking and why - this builds user trust and demonstrates disciplined workflow adherence
anti_patterns: Rationalizing that a task is too simple to warrant skill lookup - simple tasks often have overlooked complexity
Checking files or running commands before checking skills - skills define how to explore and gather information
Relying on memory of what a skill says instead of invoking it - skills evolve and current versions may have updated guidance

faq.question: What if I am 100% certain no skill applies to my question?
faq.answer: If there is truly zero chance (not 1%) that any skill applies, you can proceed without checking. However, this is rare - most tasks benefit from at least checking for workflow or domain skills.

faq.question: Does checking for skills apply to clarifying questions?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill check happens before any response, including clarifying questions. Skills often define what information to gather, so checking first ensures you ask the right questions.

faq.question: What if a skill I invoked turns out not to be relevant?
faq.answer: Once you invoke and read a skill, you can determine it does not apply. The key is checking first - you cannot know a skill is irrelevant until you read its current content.

faq.question: How do I access skills in environments without a Skill tool?
faq.answer: This skill is designed primarily for Claude Code which has native Skill tool support. In other environments, consult platform documentation for skill loading mechanisms or manually reference skill documentation.

faq.question: What is the difference between rigid and flexible skills?
faq.answer: Rigid skills (like TDD workflows or debugging checklists) must be followed exactly as written. Flexible skills (like design patterns) provide principles to adapt to context. The skill itself indicates which category it belongs to.

faq.question: Can I skip skill lookup for urgent or time-sensitive tasks?
faq.answer: No. Urgent tasks often benefit most from disciplined approaches. Skills exist to prevent costly mistakes from rushed, undisciplined work. The few seconds spent checking for skills can save significant rework time.
